rt_distribution.no_canonical_range_reason: No meta-analysis or consensus review reports ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameter ranges for working-memory tasks as a class. RT-shape work in this literature is dominated by primary studies tied to specific paradigms (n-back, Sternberg, change detection) rather than aggregated review-level norms.

between_subject_sd.no_canonical_range_reason: No meta-analytic review reports between-subject SDs of ex-Gaussian RT parameters for working-memory tasks. Reviews summarize accuracy/capacity variability, not RT-shape variability.

citations.title: The magical number 4 in short-term memory: A reconsideration of mental storage capacity
citations.quote: The capacity limit was found to be approximately 4 items (with a range of about 3 to 5) when the use of long-term memory and rehearsal mechanisms was prevented.

citations.authors: Luck, S. J., & Vogel, E. K.
citations.title: Visual working memory capacity: from psychophysics and neurobiology to individual differences
citations.quote: The capacity of visual working memory is typically estimated to be 3-4 items in young healthy adults across a wide range of stimuli and procedures.

set_size_effect_rt_slope_ms_per_item.no_canonical_range_reason: Sternberg-style linear set-size RT slopes (~30-40 ms/item) are reported in primary studies and textbook summaries, but no recent meta-analysis aggregates the slope across studies/samples to produce a canonical range. Excluded to avoid relying on primary-study values.

citations.authors: Jaeggi, S. M., Buschkuehl, M., Perrig, W. J., & Meier, B.
citations.title: The concurrent validity of the N-back task as a working memory measure
citations.table_or_figure: Results / Discussion summary across reviewed studies
citations.quote: Performance on the 2-back condition in young healthy adults across the reviewed studies typically falls in the 70-90% accuracy range, with substantial individual variability.

n_back_dprime_2back.no_canonical_range_reason: No meta-analysis provides an aggregated d' range for the 2-back task; reviews discuss d' as a recommended scoring metric but do not publish a canonical numeric range.

lag1_autocorr.no_canonical_range_reason: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation in working-memory tasks has not been characterized in any meta-analysis or review specific to this class.

post_error_slowing.no_canonical_range_reason: Post-error slowing reviews (e.g., Danielmeier & Ullsperger 2011) focus on conflict and choice-RT paradigms; no review reports a canonical PES magnitude for working-memory paradigms specifically.

proactive_interference_effect.no_canonical_range_reason: Proactive interference is well-documented qualitatively in WM reviews (e.g., Oberauer et al. 2018 benchmarks), but those reviews enumerate phenomena rather than publish a canonical effect-size range across samples.

citations.authors: Oberauer, K., Lewandowsky, S., Awh, E., Brown, G. D. A., Conway, A., Cowan, N., Donkin, C., Farrell, S., Hitch, G. J., Hurlstone, M. J., Ma, W. J., Morey, C. C., Nee, D. E., Schweppe, J., Vergauwe, E., & Ward, G.
citations.title: Benchmarks for models of short-term and working memory
citations.table_or_figure: Benchmark list (Section 'Effects of similarity and interference')
citations.quote: Proactive interference is a robust benchmark phenomenon in working memory: performance on the current list/trial is impaired by similar items from preceding lists/trials.

serial_position_primacy_recency.no_canonical_range_reason: Primacy and recency are listed as benchmark phenomena in Oberauer et al. (2018) but no canonical numeric range for the magnitude of the bow-shaped serial position curve is published at the meta-analytic level.

citations.title: Benchmarks for models of short-term and working memory
citations.quote: Serial recall produces a bow-shaped serial position curve with primacy and recency components; this is a defining benchmark for any model of short-term memory.
